content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 507 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 10, 1974

#79-113

Thomas M. Powers
Powers, Bartke & Russ
4101 MacDonald Avenue
Richmond, CA 94805

Dear Mr. Powers:

Thank you for your letter of December 13, 1978, requesting
advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act. I hope the following advice provided
pursuant to Government Code Section 91313 is helpful.

The facts as I understand them are as follows: You are a
supervisor for your county. Your law firm supervises property
You are a partner in a law firm and a real estate broker. You
and your partners occasionally practice criminal law in the
courts of County, State and other county. Some of these cases
are assigned to partners as associates of your firm by Superior
and Municipal Court judges. Local fees for these cases are
set by court order according to a schedule of attorney's fees
set by the judges. You personally do not accept any court
appointed cases.

You ask whether the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act prohibit you from handling the cases of
private litigants or prohibit your partners and associates from
handling court appointed cases in the Superior and Municipal
courts of Contra Costa County. The answer to this question is
no. However, you should not participate in decisions as a
supervisor regarding the funding of the court appointed attorney
program if such decisions will foreseeably have a material
financial effect on your law firm. I have enclosed Commission
Regulation 18702 which sets forth the standards for determining
whether a decision will have a material effect on a financial
interest.

Mr. Thomas M. Powers                                    Page Two
January 10, 1978


     Your second question concerns your relationship with
several title companies in the county. As a real estate
broker, you engage the narrow and title insurance services of
several title companies on behalf of your real estate clients.
From time to time, title companies represent developers on
matters before the Planning Commission and Board of Supervisors.
You ask whether you are prohibited from making, participating
in the making or using your official position to influence
decisions affecting developers represented by title companies
with which you do business. As a general rule, the answer is
no because the title companies are not a source of income to
you but rather you and your clients are a source of income to
the title companies. This would not be true, however, if you
received rates or services from the title companies on terms
different than those offered to other regular customers.

     Your third question is the same as the second question
except that the title company which appears before the board
on behalf of a developer is a source of income to you. Under
these facts you are disqualified from making, participating in
the making or using your official position to influence a
decision of the Board of Supervisors in three situations. First,
you are disqualified if the amount of your income from the title
company is in some way tied to the decisions rendered by the
Board of Supervisors. Second, you are disqualified if there
is a nexus between your retained and a decision of the Board
of Supervisors. Third, you are disqualified from participation
in the decision if one of its foreseeable effects will be to
increase or decrease the title company's business by the amounts
specified in Regulation 18702(a)(1). Such a change could be
brought about either directly, for example by raising the number
of escrows handled by the title company, or indirectly, for
example if the representation of such clients before the board
is a regular and significant part of the title company's
business, its success rate before the board could have a sub-
stantial effect on the amount of such business brought to the
company. See Regulation 18702(a)(3).

     If I may be of further assistance, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

                                        [signature]
By: Sarah T. Cameron
     Counsel
     Conflicts of Interest Division

Enclosure
TP:nrw

TOWER, BARTLETT & RUSS
[illegible]
San Francisco, CA [illegible]
Telephone [illegible]

December 15, 1978

WILLIAM H. BARRETT
BARBARA RUSS
DONALD C. ABBOTT
WILLIAM H. PARKER
S.P. LEE
ALEXANDER DE SAMUELSEN

LEON CLAUDE SAMPSEON
LEGAL ASSISTANT

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Legal Staff

Re: Written opinion on potential conflicts
of interests for elected office holder.

Gentlemen:

This letter is to request of you your written
opinion regarding a questioned potential legal conflict
that I may have as a member of the Contra Costa County
Board of Supervisors, which office I assume on January 9,
1979, in conjunction with my legal and real estate
practices in the same county.

To give you a little background information, I
am a senior partner in the firm of Powers, Bartlett & Russ,
which has seven lawyers, consisting of three partners,
three associates, and one employee. I actively practice
law in the City of Richmond, Contra Costa County, Califor-
nia. In addition thereto, I am a licensed real estate
broker actively involved in real estate sales, trans-
actions, and having one sales person.

The issues I am concerned about are:

1. I occasionally, and my partners and associates
occasionally, practice criminal law in the courts of
Contra Costa County and the surrounding counties. Some
of the cases are assigned to partners or associates by the
judges of the Superior and Municipal courts in this county
and are paid for by county funds by the Audit-controller
of Contra Costa County. Those funds are from the county
general fund and are paid by order of the court based upon
a court schedule of attorney's fees as by the Municipal
and Superior Courts judges solely. I personally do not
accept court appointed cases, although I do occasionally
accept misdemeanor cases from privately retained clients.

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 15, 1978
Page Two

In view of the Appellate Court's decision in People v.
Municipal Court, 69 CA3d 714, and other related cases,
my question is to you whether or not, as a member of
the Board of Supervisors, there are conflicts in the
following situations:

a. When associates, partners, or an employee
of the firm are appointed to represent indigent criminal
defendants?

b. When I represent privately retained clients
in criminal cases in the courts of Contra Costa County?

2. Part of my business as a real estate broker
involves my using various title companies doing business
in Contra Costa County for the use of their escrow and
title insurance services for my real estate clients. In
view of the fact that the title companies do, on occasion,
represent developers and builders in development and
planning activities at the county level, do I as a
member of the Board of Supervisors, have a conflict of
interest in this situation if a development or planning
activity comes before the Board of Supervisors for a
decision in which one of those title companies with which
I do business represents the developer or builder?

3. I personally, and members of the firm, have
in the past and are presently representing two title
companies doing business in Contra Costa County on activities
such as termination of title insurance questions and litigation, collection of outstanding obligations to those said
title companies, and other litigation activities for title
companies. As a member of the Board of Supervisors, do I
have a conflict of interest in the following situations:

a. When a title company which I have represented
has a matter before the Board of Supervisors in a status
of representing a developer or builder?

b. If I abstain from voting on any issue in
which a title company which I have or do represent has
a matter before the Board of Supervisors?

With respect to your opinion, I would appreciate
your conclusion of any citations of authority in writing,
as well as your opinion as to the issues of potential conflicts
I have raised. In addition, if you are in a position to advise
me of the means or methods by which I could avoid these

Fair Political Practices Commission
December 15, 1978
Page Three

conflicts, please include that in your opinion also.

Respectfully submitted,

[signature]

THOMAS M. POWERS

TMP:kc

sections.question: Whether the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act prohibit handling cases of private litigants or partners/associates from handling court appointed cases; whether the requester is prohibited from making, participating in, or using official position to influence decisions affecting developers represented by title companies with which the requester does business; and whether disqualification applies when a title company is a source of income to the requester.
sections.conclusion: No conflict prohibits handling private litigant cases or partners accepting court-appointed cases, but the Supervisor must not participate in supervisorial decisions regarding court-appointed attorney funding if such decisions will foreseeably have material financial effect on the law firm. Title companies that are not income sources present no conflict as a general rule unless special rates/services are provided. However, when a title company is an income source, disqualification applies in three situations: (1) if income is tied to Board decisions, (2) if there is nexus between retainer and Board decisions, or (3) if a foreseeable effect of the decision will increase or decrease the title company's business by amounts specified in Regulation 18702(a)(1), whether directly or indirectly through effects on the company's representation business.
sections.question_synthetic: A County Supervisor who is a law partner and real estate broker asks whether the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit his law firm from accepting court-appointed criminal cases, whether his real estate brokerage work with title companies creates conflicts when those companies appear before the Board of Supervisors on behalf of developers, and whether abstention cures any conflicts of interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Supervisor may handle private criminal cases and his partners may accept court appointments without conflict, but he must recuse from supervisorial decisions affecting court-appointed attorney funding. Title companies that pay the Supervisor create disqualification only if Board decisions have material foreseeable effects on their business, directly or through effects on their representation practice before the Board.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the original incoming request (dated December 15, 1978) and the FPPC response letter (dated January 10, 1979). The question and conclusion sections are reconstructed from the response letter's discussion of the facts and legal conclusions. No formal Q&A headers present, but clear question/answer structure in the advisory opinion. High confidence in extraction due to clear advisory nature and factual recitation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act prohibit handling cases of private litigants or partners/associates from handling court appointed cases; whether the requester is prohibited from making, participating in, or using official position to influence decisions affecting developers represented by title companies with which the requester does business; and whether disqualification applies when a title company is a source of income to the requester.

CONCLUSION: No conflict prohibits handling private litigant cases or partners accepting court-appointed cases, but the Supervisor must not participate in supervisorial decisions regarding court-appointed attorney funding if such decisions will foreseeably have material financial effect on the law firm. Title companies that are not income sources present no conflict as a general rule unless special rates/services are provided. However, when a title company is an income source, disqualification applies in three situations: (1) if income is tied to Board decisions, (2) if there is nexus between retainer and Board decisions, or (3) if a foreseeable effect of the decision will increase or decrease the title company's business by amounts specified in Regulation 18702(a)(1), whether directly or indirectly through effects on the company's representation business.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a Contra Costa County Supervisor who is a law partner and real estate broker seeking advice on conflicts of interest involving court-appointed criminal cases, title company relationships, and decisions before the Board of Supervisors. The FPPC provides guidance on when recusal is required based on financial nexus between the Supervisor's private interests and foreseeable effects of supervisorial decisions.
